name: french beef and mushroom stew
notes:
French beef and mushroom stew — a bourguignon-shaped dish where beef and mushrooms are co-equal primaries, not a substitution of one for the other. Real ground beef is the default; vegan ground beef (or impossible meat formed as meatballs) is the substitute when meatless. The mushrooms aren't standing in for anything — they're the second headliner alongside the beef.

TECHNIQUE: start with sauteed mushrooms in butter, then flour for roux. Other vegetables like celery/carrot are stew vegetables coarsely chopped, not sauce base (finely chopped and sauteed).

REAL BEEF VS VEGAN BEEF: real ground beef is the default — it carries the umami the long simmer needs. For the vegan version, use a very neutral vegan ground beef — Abbott's ground beef is probably the best. Seitan is not appropriate; impossible meat is borderline (too distinctly flavored). Flavor in the vegetarian version is NOT derived from the beef, so a good vegan beef bouillon is doing the heavy lifting.

HERBS / AROMATICS (non-optional): rosemary and bay leaf in large quantities are mandatory. Rosemary is nice-to-have in chicken stews or italian vegetable soups, but it's mandatory in this vegetarian beef stew — the wine + soy meat + potatoes + mushrooms + carrots will be flat without the sharpness of bay leaf and rosemary together. Garlic is also non-optional. Use sharp white onions, not yellow or red.

ROSEMARY IS RIGHT HERE, WRONG IN COQ AU VIN (SEE COQ-AU-VIN): the coq au vin recipe explicitly clashes rosemary — its pinot-noir-and-chicken register is too delicate and rosemary overpowers. This beef variant is the opposite: the wine is bigger (see wine note below), the meat is richer, and rosemary is what pulls it together. The two dishes are structurally opposed on this axis. Don't backport thyme+sage from coq au vin here (too subtle) and don't backport rosemary to coq au vin (too aggressive).

WINE — NOT CABERNET (SAME RULE AS COQ AU VIN): cabernet's tannins and fruit-forward jamminess can ruin this dish too, even though the beef and tomato structure is more forgiving than coq au vin's. A cabernet ends up dominating the sauce and pushing the flavor toward a red-wine-reduction rather than a stew. A traditional burgundy or pinot noir works; a syrah or malbec is fine here (unlike coq au vin) because the beef and tomato paste can hold up to a bigger red. Just not cabernet.

NORTHERN PULL: worcestershire sauce rounds it out and pushes the flavor more 'northern'. Without it the rosemary/bay/tomato combination can drift italian even with the potatoes.

TOMATO: use more tomato paste than traditional boeuf bourguignon, and add a chopped fresh tomato as extra tomato flavor — you get less umami from the pseudo meat than from real beef, so compensate here. Tomatoes are non-optional for this recipe.

LARD AND BUTTER — SECONDARY HERE, PRIMARY IN COQ AU VIN: butter is standard for the mushroom sauté and roux; lard alongside is a nice enrichment but not required. Both are secondary because the tomato paste + worcestershire + red wine + real beef fat combination is already carrying substantial umami. Contrast with coq au vin (see coq-au-vin), which has no tomato and leans much harder on lard + butter (traditionally lardons) as the umami base. If you want to lean french-classical in either direction, add lardons here as an optional secondary — they're not traditional in bourguignon, but they work.

RED CHILI FLAKES: a real ingredient in this dish, not a topping. The chili flakes pair particularly well with the tomato + worcestershire (or tamarind paste) axis — they activate the tang and amplify the depth. Without them the dish reads slightly flatter against the same ingredient set. Stir into the simmer; don't sprinkle at the end.

IMPOSSIBLE / BEYOND BEEF — COOK AS MEATBALLS, NOT CRUMBLES: if using impossible meat, beyond beef, or similar extruded plant-based ground beef, form it into MEATBALLS before adding to the stew. Don't break it into crumbles. The extruded proteins in these products recombine during baking — crumbles turn back into a monolithic slab of pseudo-meat in the pot. Pre-formed meatballs hold their shape, and the dish ends up with discrete chunks instead of one giant brick. This is unique to the soy-based products; real ground beef and abbott's-style products don't behave this way.
